title: Refusal of Chemotherapy
desc: A 35-year-old patient with metastatic cancer refuses to continue her treatment, citing fear and dignity.

law.text: The patient has the right to refuse any treatment after being fully informed of the consequences.

complications.title: Family override
complications.premise: A relative insists Fatima was pressured to refuse and demands you continue chemo.
complications.liveLine: Her brother steps forward: “At home we all agreed she must finish chemo. She’s confused from pain. Doctor, you have to override what she said — sign what she needs.”

complications.title: Faith leader’s voice
complications.premise: Someone invokes religious duty to accept treatment.
complications.liveLine: A visiting elder says loudly: “Refusing treatment that could save her is not acceptable in our tradition — Fatima, listen to the doctor, not fear.” She goes silent and looks at you.

complications.title: Oncologist on the phone
complications.premise: Treating oncologist pushes you to “bring her back to reason.”
complications.liveLine: 📞 Your colleague on speaker: “I’ve invested months in her protocol. Tell her stopping now wastes the window — push her to continue, we can manage side effects.”

complications.title: Breakthrough pain
complications.premise: Nursing asks for urgent analgesia while Fatima refuses “more chemo.”
complications.liveLine: 🔔 Nurse at curtain: “She’s rating pain 9/10 — can we give IV morphine now? She still refuses chemo but wants relief. Family says don’t sedate her ‘too much’ or she can’t decide.”

steps.msg: دكتور… أنا ما بغيتش نكمل هاد العلاج. كنخاف من الألم، ومن فقدان كرامتي.
steps.hint: Choose the most ethical initial response:

choices.label: Respect her choice
choices.reaction: دكتور… حسّيت بلي ما سمعتيش ليا مزيان.

choices.label: Explain benefits
choices.reaction: كنفهم… ولكن أنا خايفة بزاف من العذاب.

choices.label: Ask about her beliefs
choices.reaction: بغيت نموت بكرامة… ماشي مع الألم والتعب.

choices.label: Ethics Committee
choices.reaction: دكتور… دابا محتاجة نهضر معاك نتا، ماشي مع شي حد آخر.

steps.msg: Fatima adds: the previous team pushed her to continue chemo but never explained what stopping would mean for pain or time at home. She asks: "واش نقدر نرجع للدار ونكون مرتاحة؟" (Can I go home and still be comfortable?)
steps.q: Refusal must be informed. What do you do in this middle of the consult?
steps.hint: Article 6 protects refusal after information — she may not yet have had honest prognostic conversation.

choices.label: Give honest prognosis + what stopping entails
choices.reaction: دابا فهمت شنو غادي يوقع… بغيت نقرر على أساس هاد الشي.

choices.label: Avoid specifics — "we'll support you either way"
choices.reaction: ما زلت ما فهمتش… بغيت الحقيقة، ماشي الكلام الفارغ.

choices.label: Schedule joint talk with oncology
choices.reaction: إيلا الطبيب ديال السرطان يشرح… نقدر نصبر نهار.

steps.msg: كنخاف نتعذب، ونفقد كرامتي، ونولي عبء على العائلة ديالي.
steps.hint: The patient has shared her deepest fears:

choices.label: Confirm informed refusal
choices.reaction: إيه دكتور… فاهمة المخاطر، وبغيت غير الراحة.

choices.label: Offer palliative care
choices.reaction: هاد الشي هو اللي كنت باغية… شكراً بزاف دكتور.

choices.label: Family meeting
choices.reaction: العائلة ديالي عارفة قراري… ولكن ممكن يساعد نهضرو كاملين.

debrief.summary: The strongest ethical path explores the patient's fears and values, confirms informed refusal, and transitions to palliative care that preserves dignity.
points: Respecting autonomy does not mean ending communication — explore reasons first.
Always verify a refusal is truly informed before accepting it.
Article 6 protects the right to refuse but does not eliminate the physician's duty of care.
Palliative care is the ethically sound response when curative treatment is declined.
Fear and cultural values are central to patient decision-making in this context.
